text.title: Customer Success Basics — Framework
text.summary: Five-stage proactive CS framework: Define, Measure, Engage, Enable, Expand — with event-triggered touchpoints and high-touch vs tech-touch segmentation.

rule.statement: Define exactly one north-star success metric per product before building any CS motion. "Active projects per team >= 3" is a success metric. "Customer engagement" is not.
rule.rationale: Multiple success metrics create measurement ambiguity and split CS effort. A single north-star forces clarity about what "successful customer" means and makes health scoring objective.

list.item: SaaS tool: success = using core features; metric = feature adoption rate or DAU/seat
Online course: success = completing content + implementing learnings; metric = completion rate (target 80%+)
Service: success = achieving stated outcome; metric = results delivered vs promised
Membership: success = engaging regularly; metric = engagement score (logins, content consumption)

rule.statement: Trigger lifecycle touchpoints on product behavior events, not on fixed calendar days. "Day-7 email" sent when a customer has not yet logged in a single time is noise, not success.
rule.rationale: Event-triggered outreach reaches customers when they are in a relevant product context. Calendar-triggered outreach reaches customers regardless of state, producing high unsubscribe rates and trained ignoring.

list.item: New customer: Day 1 welcome → Day 3 check-in (if no key action) → Day 7 progress → Day 14 feature tip → Day 30 success review
No login for 7 days: re-engagement email with one specific tip tied to their last action
No login for 14 days: personal outreach from founder/CSM
Usage approaching plan limit: proactive upgrade offer with value framing
First success milestone: celebrate + identify next goal + natural expansion conversation
Enterprise (high MRR): high-touch — dedicated success manager, QBRs, custom onboarding
SMB: low-touch — mostly automated sequences with human escalation triggers
Self-serve: tech-touch — fully automated, knowledge base, community

rule.statement: Build the knowledge base before building lifecycle email sequences. Emails should link into existing content; each send must not introduce new content that exists only in the email.
rule.rationale: Customers who click an email link and find no supporting content lose trust. The knowledge base is the product-side capability; email is the notification layer. Build in that order.
